The robotic view shows the surgeon performing lymph node dissection, which is typically done after the prostatectomy and before the anastomosis, but was completed earlier in this case.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to access the seminal vesicles (SVs), demonstrating a technique that may differ from other approaches.

The surgeon begins the posterior dissection by identifying the line where the yellow perirectal fat meets the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder, approximately one centimeter above this junction.

The surgeon lifts the left seminal vesicle (SV) and works on the downside, following the medial aspect around the corner to the tip. Then, the SV is pulled down into the contralateral shoulder, revealing the lateral aspect for further dissection.

And I don't use clips. Some people ask, you know, why don't you use clips? Are you worried about nerves and whatnot? And we did use clips for a little while and we found we used the little five millimeter clips and we really didn't see a difference. But really the goal should be that you should be working right on the wall of the SV. So really just hugging the SV completely and then just following this lateral aspect every so often adjusting the fourth arm.

And the fourth arm management here is really critical. If you feel like you're working in a hole when you do the SVs back here, it's usually because the retraction is not adequate. You have to adjust your retraction. So when I'm having my fellows do this, that's oftentimes if they're struggling, that's oftentimes the reason is just that their retraction is not adequate. So I'll tell them, no, you got to change your retraction. So with adequate retraction, you know, this is a very doable, even on the most obese of patients, this is a very doable way to do the SV.

And I think a really a nice, nice way to access the SVs. Now, The only time that I won't do the SVs from this direction would be, for example, if a patient has had an LAR or for some reason has tons of adhesions down in the cul-de-sac and I don't want to have to work on the bowels and pull the bowels out and try to get into this plane, then I would do the SVs the old-fashioned way, you know, from dropping the bladder or coming through the bladder neck and then do them anteriorly.

But that's obviously a very rare scenario, so I would say probably Since I started doing it this direction, I've maybe had to do it two or three times the other direction. But I think it's good to know both. It's good to be comfortable with both.

The other thing is we're doing this case on a pneumo of six. So this is my routine. We've been doing this now for about five hundred cases. We do the prostates all at a pneumo of six, really all pelvic surgery, so bladders as well as cystectomies. We do them at a pneumo of six. And so you'll notice here that things will seem like they're not quite as stretched out as what you're used to when you use a pneumo of ten, fifteen, twelve, whatever you usually use.

So you wouldn't have to do that, of course. I mean, if you were doing it. Are you using the air seal system with that? Yes. Yeah, we're using air seal at six, exactly. So what we found is, you know, my fellow just presented our comparison of three hundred at six versus three hundred at fifteen, what we used to do. And what we found is that, you know, the The air seal allows you to do this ultra-low pressure without losing pneumo every time the assistant sucks.

But just the fact that you're able to do it at a pneumo of six is really quite helpful in several ways. One is that the patient has less peritoneal stretch, so it seems like the patients tend to have less pain. And that was what our data showed. Our pain scores were less at the pneumo of six. And then the shoulder pain that people always get after laparoscopic surgery, it really eliminated it. We just didn't see it anymore. We don't see that shoulder pain That people always complain about after you know, we still see with our kidneys because we don't do them at six But really on the prostates we don't see it We also don't see that scrotal swelling that we used to see when we did it at higher pneumo pressure So that pneumo of six really has solved several problems for us So we've really made it our routine again.

We've done it for about five hundred cases, you know consecutive cases We've not had to you know, adjust it regardless of obesity or anything And then the other thing is some people might ask, well, do you get more bleeding at a pneumo of six, you know, venous bleeding? And the answer is really no, not a clinically significant amount. You know, we've not transfused any patient yet who we've done at six. So, you know, hundreds of patients later, we've never had to transfuse anybody. And, you know, really what I like about it is that if it's going to bleed post-operatively, it's going to bleed at six. So when I leave the operating room, and there's no bleeding and things are dry, then I'm not worried that when the patient's in recovery and on the floor with a pneumo of zero that they might bleed because I was looking at it at six, and if they're going to bleed at six, they're going to bleed at zero. If they're not bleeding at six, they're probably not going to bleed at zero.

But if you have a pneumo of twelve or fifteen, well, then, you know, you might worry postoperatively that some big vein was kind of let loose and might cause trouble. So I really think from a bleeding point of view, I'm not worried about the ultra low pressure. If anything, I think it's helping us. I know at our institution, the anesthesiologists have been extremely happy with us switching to air seal and lowering our pressures. We've been doing it about at ten, but after seeing your fellows' data, I want to go even lower, push that limit. Are your anesthesiologists pretty excited about it, Phil?

Yeah, I mean, like you said, they noticed a huge difference when we went to air seal number one, but then there have been patients that I've had where the pneumo of six was enabling, meaning that the patient would not have tolerated a high pneumo pressure either because of COPD or just very, very obese patients. We wouldn't have been able to do the cases at a pneumo, you know, up ten or fifteen, but doing them at six was enabling for us to be able to offer them you know, surgery as opposed to radiation. So, you know, and also for the cystectomy patients, as you know, Clayton, these guys are all heavy smokers.

But I think the robotic approach is huge. They do so much better postoperatively, but just getting them through the operation with the pneumo peritoneum and the Trendelenburg, the pneumo of six again has been enabling in more than one case.

So there's no digging. If you dig, you'll be more likely to accidentally get into the prostate. So those are my tips is just keep the cautery setting low and then just float. So people watch, you know, surgeons doing the bladder neck and all they're noticing is what the right hand is doing, the cutting. But you got to notice what the left hand is doing because it's just as critical. You know, keeping it on stretch is critical to getting these muscle fibers to define themselves and to make sure that, you know, we're not getting into the actual prostate.

Hey, Ronnie, I have a comment. I think one of the nice things watching you operate, it's really nice, and a key teaching point is that you can see both tips well. Your scissors are turned where you can see both tips exposed. You're just floating over and working with the two hands. But, I mean, I think a lot of people that are starting out, they'll have the scissors or the right hand start too medially, then they'll lose their tip, like shooting a gun, and they won't be able to see the tips, and they'll pass a point easily. Yeah, another point I would make for those who are not aware is that, you know, when you use the scissor, again, if you're kind of a newer user of the robot, when you use the cautery

if you want to coag something, then use both tips. If you want to just be more cutting, you can just use one tip. So less char, in other words. So that's why I'm using one tip to cut. But if I see a bleeder, I'll go to like this and I'll use both tips. It's going to give you more char, though. So it's sort of like the difference between a needle tip bovie and a regular bovie. It just concentrates the current more when you use one prong of the scissor, as opposed to both. When you use both, you're going to get more coagging and more charring. So that's why I like to cut with one prong, which I think, you know, most people do as well. So Ronnie, this is Jay here. to get to the vas and the seminal vesicles. Yeah, so you don't want to be too close to the base of the prostate when you get back to the SVs. You don't want to be right where the SV comes in, where the vas and the SV comes into the prostate. So you want to be a little bit away from that. But since we did them first, we're going to have a space back here. But if we hadn't done them first, then we'd basically be looking for fat. Once we get to fat, then we know to start looking for the SV and the vas. So that's telling us that we're through the through the bladder muscle. So we're going to see a space here when we do it, because we already dissected it out posteriorly. But if we didn't, we'd just start seeing fat here. That's telling us, OK, I'm through the bladder muscle now. I need to start looking for the vas and the SV. But again, you don't want to be too close to the prostate when you do that. You want to be a little bit further back from that point. Ronnie, so if you were in the event to have like a buttonhole situation here, how do you repair that?

Do you repair that immediately when you identify it or do you wait until your anastomosis? So I've only had that happen once so far. And I think the key is prevention. The key to preventing, you know, a buttonhole is, you know, again, all the things that we're talking about, the left hand giving you traction, One thing that you didn't see me doing on this case that, you know, is something that you should really think about doing when you're doing the posterior bladder neck is every so often you can go and look inside here and figure out what direction is the bladder going, what direction is the prostate going here. Because sometimes what will happen is that the bladder will go way under the prostate. You'll have like a big jutting lobe here, and the bladder will go way under. And that's the time when you buttonhole because you're coming straight down thinking that the bladder is like this one where it was a shelf. but actually the bladder is going down like this, and then you make a hole in the backside of the bladder. The way to prevent that is that every so often you just look in here and see, well, which way is the bladder going?

So if the bladder is going straight down, then you keep cutting straight down. If the bladder looks like it's going away from you, then you've got to follow it. So I think prevention is the key. But if you do get a buttonhole, you know, again, I've only had it happen once, but I repaired it immediately. And then I saw Renee Sotelo talking about this at the NARIS meeting. And his recommendation was to fix it from the inside of the bladder, which I'm not sure you have to do. I don't think most people do that. But that was his recommendation just to prevent a ureteral injury. But if you do it in the midline, if you're in the midline when you get the buttonhole, I think you're going to be well enough away from the ureters that you don't have to really worry about a ureteral injury. So, you know, the time that it happened to me, I just took a three-oh Vicrol and I just did a figure of eight and closed it up. And it really shouldn't affect your catheter time either. You know, you can take your catheter out at the same time you were planning to. I mean, it looks huge on the screen, but if you buttonhole the bladder, it's going to be like maybe a centimeter at most. It's not going to affect your leak rate. So not the end of the world when it happens. Thank you, Ronnie.

But I still have some concerns about the positive margin rate, according to the literature from the randomized trial that Henry Ford just put out. I think in the field of robotic surgery, we've had a lot of innovations that people have come up with over the years, obviously. And some things have panned out and some things have not. Some things have turned out. They worked well for one surgeon. And then when they did a multi-institutional trial, they actually didn't find any difference with it. You know, the SP tube was one thing that, you know, Henry Ford found a big difference, you know, for their patient population. But then when you saw the randomized trials, you saw, you know, conflicting information. So, you know, I'm not somebody who jumps on the bandwagon too fast, I would say. I try to be a little bit cautious and kind of, you know, kind of wait and see approach and see what ends up happening.
